seoTitle: How to Clear Cloudflare Cache (2026 Guide)
metaDescription: Purge stale cached files in Cloudflare using Custom Purge for specific URLs or Purge Everything to reset your whole site's edge cache instantly.
intro: When updates to your site aren't showing up for visitors, cached copies on Cloudflare's edge servers are often the culprit. This guide covers purging specific files with Custom Purge or clearing everything at once with a full reset.

steps.heading: Open the Caching directory
steps.body: Select your domain and navigate to the Caching and Configuration section in the sidebar.

steps.heading: Choose Custom Purge for specific files
steps.body: Select Custom Purge to target specific page paths, hostnames, or asset URLs rather than clearing everything.

steps.heading: Enter the URLs to purge
steps.body: Type the specific asset paths or hostnames into the form fields to isolate exactly what gets cleared.

steps.heading: Or use Purge Everything for a full reset
steps.body: If you need a complete reset, locate the 'Purge Everything' button to clear all stored assets across every edge server.

steps.heading: Confirm the purge
steps.body: Review the warning about potential origin server load during a full purge, and confirm to complete the action.

faq.question: Will Purge Everything slow down my site temporarily?
faq.answer: It can, since your origin server has to regenerate and re-cache content that was previously served instantly from the edge, causing a short-term load increase.

faq.question: How is Custom Purge different from Purge Everything?
faq.answer: Custom Purge lets you clear specific files or paths, while Purge Everything wipes all cached assets across your entire site at once.

faq.question: How often should I clear my Cloudflare cache?
faq.answer: Most site changes don't require a manual purge if your caching rules and origin headers are set up correctly, so reserve manual purges for when updates aren't showing up as expected.

faq.question: Does clearing the cache affect my DNS settings?
faq.answer: No, cache purging only clears stored file copies at the edge and has no effect on your DNS records or configuration.
